title: Bluey Phone Review

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Hey, what's up? I'm MKBHD here, and welcome to a hands-on and first impressions of the Bluey phone made by VTech.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So, I was just scrolling top-rated phones on Amazon a couple days ago, and I saw this one has gotten incredible ratings, and I haven't gotten my hands on it yet. Maybe I'm not on their review program list or something.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So, I ordered one, and now it's here. It costs $10.99. That's $10.99.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So, first of all, the unboxing experience is interesting. The phone actually ships exposed in this open-faced cardboard box, which is unique. I don't think I've ever seen that before.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But then actually getting the rest of the way into the box was kind of unintuitive and difficult, to be honest. I had to rip some cardboard to get to these fasteners behind the phone holding it in and untwist those. Didn't feel very premium, but I suppose that's forgivable at this price.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then there is no charger or cable included in the box. And while it's a rare 2026 phone that does have removable batteries, there's also a tool required to open the back and replace them, and that's not included either.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Thankfully, though, it does actually come with enough juice from the factory to just fire it up out of the box.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So, the phone itself is definitely on the big side, but not too big. We've seen phones like this before. It's roughly the same size as a Nexus 6—a little bit smaller, actually.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But this phone must clearly be some type of minimal phone because it's actually rocking a 1.7-inch black-and-white display up here. And despite this antenna up here at the top, there are no cell radios, no internet connection, no cameras at all, and exclusively physical buttons.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It doesn't even have Bluetooth, which in a Bluey phone, you'd think maybe it would. But you know, I do know a few people in real life that are trying to use their phone less by switching to a minimal phone.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And that makes sense, but you look at the minimal phones these days and they all still have these huge touchscreens and cameras and internet connections and apps... cowards.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So yeah, this Bluey phone, it takes it to the next level. When you type numbers on the Bluey phone, instead of making any phone calls, it just says the numbers out loud to you. So that's nice.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The speaker on the back has three volume levels and ordinarily a single rear-facing speaker wouldn't be that nice, but the grill and the opening are more than big enough to never accidentally block it with your finger. So that's thoughtful design.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It also does have a few games built-in, which might sound counterintuitive for a minimal phone, but these are much healthier than doom scrolling TikTok or Instagram or something like that. My personal favorite is the one where you can blow bubbles by blowing into the microphone. It's sweet.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Also, look how fast this boots up. This is power... already on. So one of the fastest booting phones. Bubbles right there. Okay, now it's your turn. Blow into the microphone.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: You did it!

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I did it.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It also has a chat feature built in, which again might sound backwards for a minimal phone, like 'really, chat, AI slop?' But no, it's not AI. The only person you can talk to is Bluey and Bingo the dogs.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Look, I think if other reviewers are reviewing this phone up against other more expensive flagships, they're making a mistake here. It's not gonna have the thin bezels or high benchmark scores to compete with the 13, 15, 20 dollar phones.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I'm not even sure this thing's processor can run a benchmark to be honest.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But as a minimal phone, this one really commits to that idea more than any other that I've seen. Like E-ink is cool and everything, but you could technically still find your way over to Instagram on the Boox Palma.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But if you have less than a thousand total pixels across your whole display and never go above three to four frames per second, and also have literally no cameras and no internet connection, then battery life becomes weeks instead of days, and screen time becomes minutes instead of hours, and total doomscrolling is effectively reduced to zero.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: This, this is good. This is a good minimal phone, especially for the price. It's undercutting the competition by from what I can tell quite a bit.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So good work Bluey. Thanks for watching. Catch you in the next one. Also check the date.

highlights: The Bluey phone is presented as the ultimate "minimal phone," achieving zero doomscrolling by completely omitting internet connectivity, cameras, and a high-resolution display.
Despite its low $10.99 price point, the device features a 1.7-inch black-and-white screen, physical buttons, and a design that ensures the rear speaker is never accidentally blocked.
The phone replaces modern smartphone distractions with simple, interactive games, such as a feature that allows the user to blow into the microphone to create digital bubbles.
Rather than making traditional calls or utilizing AI, the device's communication is limited to reading dialed numbers aloud and scripted chat interactions with characters.
summary:
In this transcript, renowned tech reviewer Marques Brownlee (MKBHD) provides a satirical, high-level "hands-on" review of the Bluey phone by VTech. Applying his standard professional critique to a $10.99 children’s toy, Brownlee evaluates the unboxing experience, hardware specifications, and functionality. He notes the lack of premium packaging and included accessories, such as chargers or tools to access the removable batteries, but highlights the device's large physical footprint and its 1.7-inch black-and-white display.

The core theme of the review is the device’s status as the "ultimate minimal phone." Brownlee humorously praises its lack of modern features—such as cameras, internet connectivity, Bluetooth, and cellular radios—as a bold commitment to reducing screen time. He explores unique features like voice-feedback for dialing, a fast boot-up time, and simple interactive games, such as blowing into the microphone to create digital bubbles. He argues that by offering no way to access social media or the internet, the phone effectively eliminates "doomscrolling" and outperforms high-end minimalist devices.

Ultimately, the transcript serves as a tongue-in-cheek commentary on the current trend of minimalist technology. Brownlee concludes that for its low price point, the Bluey phone provides a superior distraction-free experience compared to expensive "minimal" smartphones that still include touchscreens and apps. The review ends with a reminder to "check the date," confirming the video as an April Fools' Day parody.
